Name: Christopher B.
Comment:
Buddakan is, without a doubt, my favorite Manhattan restaurant. The culinary experience here is unparalleled, with each dish showcasing a perfect blend of flavors and creativity. From the first bite to the last, the food is a testament to the chef's expertise and dedication to excellence.
What truly sets Buddakan apart is not just the exceptional cuisine but also the ambiance that envelopes you from the moment you step inside. The restaurant's atmosphere is nothing short of magical - a perfect balance of chic elegance and vibrant energy. The stylish décor and thoughtfully designed spaces create an intimate yet lively dining environment.
The bar at Buddakan deserves special mention for its impressive array of beverages, expertly crafted cocktails, and a wine selection that complements the diverse menu impeccably. Whether you're indulging in a pre-dinner drink or enjoying a nightcap, the bar adds an extra layer of sophistication to the overall experience.
Buddakan has mastered the art of creating an unforgettable dining journey - a symphony of flavors, a captivating ambiance, and a bar that elevates the entire experience. It's not just a restaurant; it's a culinary sanctuary in the heart of Manhattan that continues to enchant and delight with every visit.

Name: Ramona G.
Comment:
At 10,300+ reviews, Halal Guys is one of the top most reviewed places in the world! I remember first hearing about Halal Guys back in 2016, my friends who are also yelpers brought me to the Halal Guys location in Costa Mesa, CA which had just opened that year. I had never had gyros before, but after trying it that night I've been hooked ever since!
For 8 years now I've been dying to visit the original Halal Guys food cart in New York and I finally got the chance a couple of months ago! Beware! There's a copycat Halal Guys on the opposite side of the block. I was shocked at first that the line wasn't that long, and when I looked over, I saw another Halal stand, it turns out we were standing in the incorrect line. The authentic Halal Guys has a super long wait. I've noticed people on Yelp posting the wrong Halal Guys photos to this page. It sucks that there's a copycat business right next to them to confuse customers. I verified with the guys working the food stand, that they have no relation or connection to the other fake Halal Guys cart a few feet away. So don't make that mistake of going to the wrong food cart. If the line seems too short, you're probably at the wrong cart. We stood outside for about 45 minutes, but to me it's worth it since I had been waiting years to visit. 
I ordered a small beef gyro plate $9 with extra white sauce. Oh my gosh, it's soooo delicious! If you've never had Halal Guys before, you'll be addicted! I'm warning you!
Reply Content: Hi Ramona, thank you for sharing your wonderful review about The Halal Guys! We are happy to have served you, please feel free to reach out to our team if you have any additional feedback.

Name: Matthew L.
Comment:
The Halal Guys is a Halal street food cart and restaurant chain that launched in Manhattan, New York in 1990. It was founded by Egyptian immigrants Mohamed Abouelenein, Ahmed Elsaka and Abdelbaset Elsayed. The three partners traveled to the United States in the 1980s with hopes for a better life and dreams of entrepreneurial success. They first started off with a hot dog food cart. By 1992 they had switched to an option that no one else was tackling: authentic Halal food! They initially catered to Muslim taxi drivers who had few options for Halal cuisine. It wasn't long before a much more diverse clientele was going crazy for the Halal Guys' delicious platters of chicken and gyro over rice, falafel, pita and famous white sauce. Although Halal food vendors can now be found all over New York City, it was the Halal Guys who originated the craze. More food carts would pop up around the city before the partners turned their attention to franchising brick & mortar locations. As of 2024, there are well over 100 Halal Guys locations spread out over five countries... but the food carts can still be found at the intersection of W 53rd Street and 6th Avenue in Manhattan. 
My family and I have been enjoying the Halal Guys' delicious food for many years now. We were very excited when a brick & mortar location opened in my home of New Haven in 2016. I visited many times until it closed in 2021 due to water damage from a burst pipe. I anxiously await the day that a new location opens in Connecticut. My family and I recently visited the original location in NYC. After a long day exploring in the city, we wanted to grab some dinner to take with us on the train back to Connecticut. When we saw the food carts, our minds were immediately made up. We decided to order and share two of their famous platters: one with falafel and one with chicken. As always, they tasted amazing; really hit the spot! We'll continue to be happy Halal Guys customers for years to come!

The white sauce is so creamy and dangerously addicting! The red sauce gives yummy punches of heat - Add with caution.
They've reliably served food come rain or shine. My visits have spanned many years apart, but the cart always satiates my hunger with the utmost satisfaction.
Service is speedy! Although, I don't mind long wait times, because I get to enjoy the aromatic steam rolling off the mountains of meat. The chaotic environment from cars honking, pedestrians yelling, or pigeons trying to sneak a snack attack from my platter becomes quite negligible once I take my first bite :)

Name: Rehnuma K.
Comment:
1/1 Ambience; 2/2 Food; 1/1 Service; 1/1 Value
The original Halal Guys was my first meal after moving to NYC 8 years ago, and despite the many changes since then, the flavors and quality here has stayed beautifully consistent. The staff is always friendly and fast, which even with long lines, keeps it moving quickly. 
The platters have always been fresh and piping hot. In complete transparency, the chicken is drier and blander to other carts. However, it's the high quality and incredible taste of the sauce combined with the juiciness of the lamb that just elevates the Halal Guys to a different echelon of flavor. They serve the sauce in packets to be poured over, so definitely ask for an extra and you won't be disappointed. I'm used to eating very spicy food, but their hot sauce is no joke even for me. Also served in individual packets, pour with caution. 
The small platters are $9, while the large are $11. They do not skimp on the portion sizes and each box is packed full. An iconic NY staple that is definitely a bang for your buck, so definitely do check this one out!
Reply Content: Hi Rehnuma, thanks for the 5-star review! We appreciate you taking the time out of your day to rate The Halal Guys.

Name: Kelsey C.
Comment:
Back to my old stumbling grounds, Halal Guys on 52nd and 6th after many a late night. A surefire way to get sober, guaranteed to have enough left over for the hangover, this used to be the best and cheapest meal in New York. 
But the meal I got, I can't even recognize. Mass-produced, lukewarm bits of beef, nothing like the freshly seared slabs of lamb-based gyro meat of my memories. He didn't even bother to sear it for me, just grabbed it out of a bin. I thought it was just the brick and mortars that were bad, but I guess nothing good stays good. Just slather on the white sauce, I guess. It still tastes pretty good, but it's like finding Burger King replaced your favorite In n' Out.
I know there are better carts out there now, but I couldn't leave New York without a bite of nostalgia. I guess there's a reason some memories should just stay that way.

Halal Guys is an iconic halal food cart in the heart of NYC. The halal food cart scene in NYC has become popular because of this food cart. The menu is pretty simple, either a platter or sandwich. The choices of protein is gyro, chicken, or a combo of both. There is also a falafel option now. What makes Halal Guys so delicious is the signature white sauce. It's creamy and tangy, and it pairs so well with the meat, rice, and lettuce. Some people like adding the hot sauce too, but I don't have the heat tolerance.
With multiple locations throughout the city, it's a convenient place to stop by, knowing that you'll get a great platter or sandwich to eat, even at night. Whether you're a local or visitor, I would definitely recommend trying Halal Guys for a classic NYC experience.

Name: Eric B.
Comment:
Gayle and I could see this Halal Guys food truck complex from our Midtown Hilton Hotel room.  And as day grew into evening, the line up was impressive. 
But first I digress with a couple questions:
What is Halal? Halal food is that which adheres to Islamic law.  That usually refers to meat and standards of slaughter.
Is Halal food also Kosher?  Not entirely, particularly that Halal allows the mixing of meat and milk, which is not Kosher.  Also, shellfish is considered Halal, but not Kosher. 
Is Kosher food also considered Halal?  Yes
Gayle and I stood in line one evening and tried out the Halal guys for ourselves.  It's largely a cash only business, although I did notice one credit card transaction.  
I had the chicken platter, while Gayle went for lamb.  White sauce, rice, pita, other fixings were included.  We took it up to our hotel room and enjoyed our meal with a couple adult beverages we had in the fridge.  It was a nice evening out in Manhattan.
Reply Content: Hi Eric, thank you for the positive comments. We are so happy you enjoyed your meal at The Halal Guys and look forward to seeing you again!

Name: Eric V.
Comment: This is a sad review for me. Ippudo was THE place. The absolute best ramen in NYC. However, at this point I don't think they are the best ramen on their block. To give you some history, I was an "Ippudo OG", an early adopter if you will. I was there on day one when they just opened and I went there as often as I could. We would sneak out from work NOWHERE NEAR Ippudo and time subways so we could enjoy a nice Akimaru Modern and get back before our lunch time was over. I didn't even know the subways had schedules until Ippudo opened. Life happened as it does and I got a new job outside of NYC. I would no longer be able to go to my beloved Ippudo. After around 10 years I finally got to come back and I wish I hadn't. I don't know what Akamaru Shinaji means in Japanese but if it means "overly expensive disappointment" it would make sense. The broth...less flavorful, the umami ball is now some red goo, the pork belly that use to be thick and fatty was now thin and tough, oh and everything else that USED to be in the modern now cost extra. I get it the world had a pandemic, the economy sucks it's hard to get supplies and they are more expensive but I spent $200 for 4 bowls of soup that are no better than ones I could get at H-Mart for $45 bucks. Sorry not happening again

Name: Jenny H.
Comment:
It was a chilly Sunday and perfect day to have Ramen. We decided to visit Ippudo since it's been years since my last visit.
I was surprised there was no line and we were quickly seated at a communal table sharing with a couple and few individuals. The place is not large, classy and tight. The dining area has counter tops, communal tables and booths for groups. 
It took a bit of time for the server to finally arrive at our table to take out order. We decided to order takoyaki and 2 classic tonkasu style Ramen.
The food arrived without too much of a wait. The dough on the takoyaki was heavier & doughier than what I'm used to and missing the bonito flakes. Instead it haa what appears to be tempura crunchies but were greasy & not necessary. I was excited for the ramen because of the broth. I can't say I was wowed as I remembered. The ramen was chewy texture and grabbed the broth well. The broth was rock, deep and sweet. But somehow the toppings seem more skkmpier and everything is added for extra. Paid $3 for tomago (soy sauce egg). Overall it was good but for what I paid I had better with us competitors. And be ready to pay at least 20% tip though not quite sure lot of services was required to bring a could bowl of noodles. Think this is my last time visiting them.

Name: Edgar L.
Comment:
Ippudo is an absolute gem in the world of tonkatsu ramen! From the moment you step in, you're greeted with a warm and inviting atmosphere that sets the stage for a culinary journey unlike any other. The staff are not only attentive but also incredibly knowledgeable about their menu, ready to guide you through their tantalizing offerings.
Now, let's talk about the star of the show: the tonkatsu ramen. It's a symphony of flavors and textures, with the rich, creamy broth balancing perfectly with the tender slices of pork and al dente noodles. Every spoonful is a delight to the senses, leaving you craving for more with each bite.
What sets Ippudo apart is their commitment to quality. You can taste the care and expertise that goes into every bowl, making it a truly authentic and unforgettable experience. Whether you're a ramen aficionado or a first-timer, Ippudo is sure to leave a lasting impression. Don't miss out on this culinary masterpiece!

Name: Irene D.
Comment:
Ippudo, established in Fukuoka, Japan in 1985, has become synonymous with top-tier tonkotsu ramen. Known for its rich, creamy broth that simmers for hours, Ippudo delivers a depth of flavor that has captivated a global audience. As the chain expanded internationally, it preserved its hallmark: a blend of traditional techniques and contemporary flavors served in a sleek, modern setting. Today, Ippudo restaurants around the world continue to draw ramen enthusiasts eager to experience its signature dishes and warm, welcoming atmosphere. 
The karaka spicy was just mediocre. The noodles were very chewy but the broth was more spicy than flavorful. The takoyaki was one of the worst I tasted. Felt like a ball of flour. 
What we ordered: 
Karaka Spicy 3.5/5
Takoyaki 2.5/5

Name: Bailey R.
Comment:
Our entire experience was phenomenal! 
I do not like ramen, but I tagged along with my husband and our friends because they all love ramen. Our friend ordered an Ippudo Veggie Bun for each of us (we're all vegetarian) which he raved about and boy, was he right!! I'd never had one of these before but now I'm excited to come back to this restaurant, just so I can eat more of these!! I had two of them and some edamame while everyone else enjoyed their ramen. 
They have 3 vegetarian ramen options for those who want to enjoy ramen without having to worry about meat. Apparently they recently changed their menu as our friend's favorite ramen is no longer on the menu, but all of the ramen eaters at my table really enjoyed what they ordered!
The ambience is low lighting with beautiful decor throughout the restaurant. The service was excellent! We were quickly taken care of by the friendly staff. 
Also, they have a very unique way of greeting guests as they are being seated! I won't spoil it, but will instead tell you to try this place to find out for yourself!

Name: Mara Y.
Comment:
I know Ippudo is a very popular place, but I don't really see the major hype. It felt like a pretty typical ramen place, but with slightly more gimmicks. 
We came on a weekday night with no reservation, and were almost immediately seated. We sat at the bar, so it was nice seeing the chefs prepare the food. Whenever someone walks into the dining room, the entire staff yells to welcome them in. It was fun the first few times, but got old pretty quickly.
The service was very good, and I don't really have any comments there. The restaurant is definitely well decorated and has a nice vibe. As far as the food goes, the appetizers were amazing, but the ramen not so much. If the apps were an 8, I'd say the ramen was more like a 5 or 6. It felt like an oddly small portion, but by no means insufficient -- I'm just used to ramen positions usually being huge. Mine was also strangely oily.
All in all, I'd say Ippudo would be a nice place to go if you're looking for ambiance, service, and an overall nicer experience, but if you're looking for amazing ramen, I think you'd be better off elsewhere.

Name: Eric T.
Comment:
Ah, Ippudo--the perennial favorite of ramen enthusiasts and noodle novices alike. With its inviting ambience and hip bar vibes, it's easy to see why this place draws a crowd. But as any seasoned critic knows, looks can be deceiving.
As a repeat visitor to Ippudo, I was hoping for redemption after a lackluster first experience. Alas, lightning didn't strike twice, and once again, I found myself in the throes of disappointment.
Let's start with the Karaka Spicy--a dish that promised an added kick to Ippudo's signature tonkotsu broth. But alas, the egg on my face wasn't from the spice; it was from the lukewarm reception my taste buds received. The chashu, touted as a highlight, fell flat with its dry and chewy texture, leaving me longing for the tender embrace of a properly cooked pork belly.
The Dreaming of You cocktail offered a glimmer of hope--a refreshing blend of IICHIKO SHOCHU and lemon sour that provided a welcome respite from the culinary letdown that preceded it. But alas, even the most tantalizing libations couldn't erase the taste of disappointment lingering on my palate.
In the end, Ippudo left me torn. While I'm a big fan of the ambience and friendly service, I can't in good conscience overlook the lackluster performance of the star attraction: the ramen. Perhaps I'm missing something, or maybe I'm just not the target audience. But for now, I'll be taking my noodle cravings elsewhere, in search of a bowl that truly satisfies.

Comment: I was very excited to try Ippudo on my recent trip to NYC as so many friends recommended this place and it has 10K+ reviews on Yelp, however, the place was tad bit disappointing! They did not have any vegan Ramen options (there are vegetarian options, their noodles have egg). We tried the Nirvana Karaka Ramen which was again, not that flattering! Our server however was sweet and he got in the ramen broth and all the ingredients separately so that my friend who doesn't eat egg noodles could mix the broth and veggies with steamed rice. We also tried Veggie buns and they were very tasty. It had fried mushrooms which was very crispy. We also tried a sparkling Sake and Suntory Old Fashioned and both the drinks were good and complimented with the food. I've had better Ramen before so this place didn't really impress me that much, I'm assuming their non-vegetarian options are better and hence people pick this place as their go-to Ramen spot. The overall ambiance and decor is cute. They have large tables and a lot of the seats are merged together so that a bigger group can be easily accommodated. It was pretty packed on a Friday late night!

I prefer the pork broth w the regular curly (more Al dente noodles, and not the Tongazu thin noodles that was just so soft and squishy!   I hate my noodles so soft.   And the broth didn't really have a sweet depth of flavor I was expecting for $27 bowl of noodles!   It is worse than what I make at home.   And it wasn't spicy enough, so my friend asked for more  sauce and we were charged an extra $4 for it.   If I am paying that much for a bowl of noodles, stop nickel-and-diming your customers!    We didn't just order a bowl of ramen, we ordered 2 appetizers to share, and a pork belly for each of us (3 of us.) 
I would never go back, it is not worth it.  And sometime the wait is insane, for what??   A mediocre bowl of noodles?    Nah, . Take your money elsewhere.   There are so many amazing places in NYC!

He overheard our table and thought we were placing our order. He interrupted and corrected one of us saying we wanted a different menu item because "that's not what it's called." Name: Bina T.
Comment:
My boyfriend and I were walking around and decided to come here. We had no idea that this place was famous in any way. We saw a ramen shop and since it was a bit chilly, we headed in.
As soon as I sat down and looked at the menu, I kind of looked around and whispered to my boyfriend, "Oh my god, we're at Ippudo!!!" I've seen this place on Tiktok and Instagram multiple times and heard about it but since I'm from LA, I never thought that we'd accidentally stumble upon it!!
I ended up ordering the Akamaru Modern ramen and my boyfriend ordered the shoyu ramen... I was lowkey blown away. Wow. The noodles were cooked to the correct al dente so it had a good bite. The broth in itself was amazing - I love how not overly fatty it was. Too much pork fat in the broth is a little too rich for me, but they nailed it right on the market.
I tried the shoyu ramen and it wasn't super salty! It had the right amount of shoyu taste and the broth was amazing as well. I was genuinely so shocked, I never thought I'd love a hyped restaurant but this restaurant deserved all the hype. 10/10 would come again.

It didn't come with a boiled egg . The egg is one of the best part of the ramen.
My server recommended me this ramen, saying it was one of the two popular ones there. I forgot what's the other one. 
But if you are looking for a more lighter ramen, the karaka spicy one isn't it. 
I'll say this ramen is rather mid. But I would still try out other ramens, maybe, but more interested in trying other ramen shops.

I had the Karaka Spicy Ramen and I absolutely loved it. The spicy mixture placed on top of the broth was the star of the dish for me. I enjoyed the chicken bun, chicken karaage and the Spicy Gyoza as well. The gyoza was my fav of all the appetizers.
I would definitely want to visit Ippudo again to try more dishes. I would recommend Ippudo to my vegetarian friends as well as the vegan ramen was equally delicious.
They do take reservations online so that's convenient. We came here on a Friday night with reservation and didn't have to wait too long to get our table.

I had the karaka spicy ramen and my friend had the akamaru shinaji. Both were delicious. The broth is rich and flavorful. They use thin noodles for their ramen noodles and I usually prefer the chewy curly ramen noodles but this was the best thin noodles I've had. I'll definitely be back for more ramen!

Comment:
Another solid ramen restaurant in NYC. This place has an abundance of reviews, so this place has to be good. I don't think Ippudo disappointed at all. I came in on a weekend night for two and was able to be sat within 20 minutes. This is pretty good for prime time seating. Service overall is fast and friendly. Ramen is great for a no frills experience. 
My boyfriend and I ordered the akamaru and shiomaru. I would recommend the akamaru more if you like ramen that really packs a punch. The extra umami flavoring is worth. Otherwise, if you want something a bit lighter but still hits the spot, the shiomaru is a great choice as well. 
There's a variety of seating for small and big groups, so I think it accommodates well. The space is also much bigger than I anticipated. I would recommend coming here for a more casual bite as it can get a little loud.

Name: Kaiju K.
Profile Location: Upper West Side, New York, NY
Comment:
This review is about the Ippudo east side, on 4th Ave. between 9th - 10th streets 
THE GIST: Ill-ease from loss of appetite from the obvious cattle call mentality of treat customers with generic-for-profit slop
There will be a point very soon into your meal where you genuinely question your feeling of ill-ease coming from either eating too much or from losing your appetite.
This could be coming from either the unprofessional use of the "F"-word by the personnel behind the bar counter reverberating around the dining room in plain sight and earshot of customers...or your instincts telling you to leave immediately may be coming from the fact that this place lacks any true "Japanese" sense in any aspect of a dining experience; sight (not one Japanese person working there that I could see anywhere...It's obvious the employees don't even like working their as blase and robotic personalities saturate each employee...this tells one quite a bit if you think a little about the reasons that a Japanese person would not want to work in Ippudo), sound was a cacophony of fake welcoming of patrons that lacked any depth of genuine "welcome", taste the pedantic and generic lack of quality in every single item on that menu, smell the air that reeks of generic-for-profit-lack of quality...and touch the side dish toppings for the ramen such as the pork and spinach and you'll be touching stale toppings that not one Japanese ramen restaurant I have been in while I lived in Japan would ever consider allowing to be a representative of their food. 
But GO! And spend the $100 for 2 to experience this bogus, cheap rouse yourself...
but one thing to think of: The original owner, not knowing where they are eating would never return...And I END with THIS WARNING: Later after dinner you will be walking and you will be left with the remains of a very unsatisfied full feeling.

Name: Anjali B.
Comment:
Went here this weekend without a reservation, we had to wait about 30 minutes. Wasn't bad as we just walked around but there is also room to wait in the front. We were seated towards the back, but honestly I don't think it matters where you sit the vibe was casual but very lively. We ordered the pork buns to start and then ramen. 
The buns were really good, would recommend. We got the first two ramens off the top of the menu (can't recall the names right now), both were so good. The second one (with garlic) was more flavorful and a little spicer, more up my alley. But honestly I don't think you could have gone wrong with anything.
Very reasonably priced and the service was quick and all the staff were very nice. Would recommend!!

Name: Christopher T.
Comment:
Sad news that Ippudo did away with their vegetarian sesame broth -- created by ramen master Fumihiro Kanegae -- that was revealed in 2016. Prior to that, there was only one vegetarian broth made from wasabi. The Wasabi Broth was great but with the introduction of the sesame equivalent -- which took three years of testing and refining -- it matched that same slurping experience as the traditional Tonkotsu Soup and the feeling on the tongue was like nothing I had experienced. The traditional broth is made by boiling pork bones for a long durations which draws out the umami flavor and converts the collagen to gelatin to give the soup that rich, creamy texture. Until the introduction of their Sesame Broth, vegetarians were sadly deprived of such an incredible experience. Worthwhile ramen experiences for vegetarians are rare so I felt so loved coming to Ippudo and being so well cared-for. 
When I stopped by for lunch today, I was disappointed to discover that the Sesame Broth had been removed from the menu. The rice noodles (available to cater to the gluten-free folks out there,) had also been yanked. 
There are still three ramen options for vegetarians (which are delicious) and they still offer their incredible Crispy Eggplant Bao so they still deserve a five-star rating. But in all honesty, I feel a bit less "seen" with their menu modifications.

Name: Jingqiao H.
Comment:
What a disappointment! Haven't come in maybe 4 or 5 years, and this is the first time after the pandemic. The bowl looked smaller and the price went up a lot. I ordered a Yuzu Shoyu Chicken ramen. No yuzu flavor at all but just a tiny bit yuzu peels. The soup was way too salty (how much soy sauce did you pour in it???) and the chicken meat was also purely saltiness and somewhat gamy (like the taste you got from undercooked/raw chicken tender). If you don't taste yuzu, don't put it in the name just because you threw a few peels in the soup,  expecting the flavor would come out so that you can charge people $20+. I was really pissed after paying $28-ish for such a low-quality bowl of noodles. My server was nice though.
Bye, Yippudo. I don't think ill be back again.

Name: Ian P.
Comment:
Well now, if you're hankerin' for a slice of the Big Apple's finest, you best amble on down to Artichoke Pizza on East 14th Street. This here establishment has a selection of by-the-slice pizzas that'll make a man pause and ponder the marvels of modern cookery. They've got ingredients so innovative, you'd think a culinary wizard conjured them up.
Now, let me regale you with a tale of their crab pizza - a concoction so delightful, it could make a riverboat gambler weep with joy. If you're keen on a flavor that's a tad out of the ordinary, this one's for you.
But mark my words, these pies are thick - thicker than a Mississippi mud pie, nearly Neapolitan in their stature. For those of you who fancy a crispier crust, like myself, you might find these a touch too hearty. Their famed artichoke slice, while rich and creamy, can be a gooey affair, a veritable challenge to eat without adornin' yourself with its luscious sauce. But I reckon if you don't mind a bit of a mess, you'll find it well worth the trouble.
In summation, Artichoke Pizza serves up slices that are as unique and hearty as a riverboat adventure. Should you find yourself in that neck of the woods, do yourself the favor of indulgin' in a slice or two.

Name: The G.
Comment:
Based on reviews here we tried Artichoke Basille.
First: a Hole in the wall place with no seating (or restroom)
Second: the "artichoke" pizza topping was essentially tasteless white glop with a few pieces of spinach and even fewer pieces of artichoke mixed in. The glob reminded me of the cheap crema used in Mexican fast food (at least places nowhere near Mexico). Just awful!  Having lived in the SF Bay Area for 30 years, my wife and I know artichokes and artichoke pizza. Never again here.
Third: place was not busy. One one customer besides us at 4:30 pm on a weekday, yet counter guy was inattentive.
Fourth: very pricey! $22 for two slices and two cans of coke.
On the good side: the slices were about 50% larger than average NYC neighborhoods slices. And my wife got a pepperoni/sausage slice which was not bad. For that pie they used the standard Stanislaus 7-11 tomato sauce. Yes we are pizza connoisseurs and know the ingredient sources for many of the NY joints.
Overall, I'd give this place a pass. At least, stay away from that "signature" pie!

Name: Ariana M.
Comment:
The artichoke pizza at this place is truly exceptional. It's reminiscent of savoring a creamy spinach artichoke dip from Trader Joe's, but in the form of a pizza. The crust is cooked to perfection, with a delightful salty and garlicky flavor that complements the toppings beautifully.
While we also tried slices of pepperoni and crab pizza, it's undeniable that the artichoke steals the spotlight. In fact, we even created a heavenly combination by sandwiching the crab and artichoke slices together. The service was excellent; the staff offered to cut each pizza into thirds for easier sharing, but we declined, opting instead for the experience of diving into a generously-sized, dripping pizza. In my opinion, it tastes even better that way.
The store offers plenty of space, but there are only standing side tables available for dining. Each pizza is served on two plates since the slice is so big.

Name: Dan A.
Comment:
I'm a big fan of Artichokes and don't see this vegetable as a topping in many pizza spots so had to try Basille.
Small and more of a takeout space with stand and eat areas as well outdoor seating.
I saw about 8 different Slice options.  The slices are huge and can easily feed two.  Ordered the Artichoke and Pepperoni/Meatball slices.  
Artichoke was very good although not what I expected.  It's a rich and decadent slice given the cream sauce and two cheeses.  Thought the sauce had a seafood taste to it but ingredients don't indicate any seafood.  More Artichokes please.  Overall, unique slice and one is more than a meal.
Pepperoni/Meatball was excellent.  Great balance of toppings and sauce and really liked the Meatball topping. 
Service was quick and friendly.  $15 min for Credit Card purchase.  Good value for money given the huge slices. Will be back to try the Crab and Vodka slices.

Since you're wondering...
Every single time I come to Artichoke, the pizza is a great. I love pizza and find that I am pretty judge-y when it comes to NYC pizza and this place never disappoints. 
I typically get a pepperoni slice. I love that it has plenty of pepperoni for each bite and that the slice is really large and in charge. The crust is think, and cooked a little burnt which is my preference, and the rest of the dough is nice and thin. 
On my last visit I also ordered an artichoke slice, had to see what was in the name, and it didn't disappoint. It was messy, probably because of the different cheeses melting together and had a decent flavor. Although good, I think I'll stick with my usual pepperoni. 
The price is where you might consider not ordering but for the size of the slice, I think its worth it. Service is usually ok, fats paced, and ambiance is typical pizzeria.

Name: Cathy G.
Comment:
So it's a non-fanciful type of eating joint - it's selling pizzas. But I do have a gripe about there being no seating - I brought my older in law in that has some walking issues and didn't realize that there wasn't going to be any chairs around - so if you're looking to chill around - not the place to be doing it unless you like standing. 
The service guys behind the counter were fine - pizza came out quick and the slices are pretty big. One pizza covers about 2 slices. But my next gripe here is that the price of a big slice is a little over 10$ - maybe 12$ ish. Which means that it's an average of a 5-6$ slice. Which is already double the average slice of a regular pizza which should be around $3 (especially since they got rid of my fave $1 slice). So that's a bummer - with new york having a plentiful amount of affordable pizza joints this blows. 
Then lastly - the most disappointing - the pizza wasn't that great. Got the signature artichoke slice - since I adore artichoke pizza slices. It was overly salty and just tasted kind of like a bland artichoke dip thing from a supermarket. My husband got a vodka slice which was the blandest thing I tasted. The crust though was actually pretty good but crust does not a pizza make. 
I'd say skip this place entirely - just not worth it - unless you're right next to it at 12 AM after hitting bars all night and are dying for a slice.

Name: Jason D.
Comment: Is $2 worth fighting for? I think so but you decide for yourself. On Friday, March 24 I was craving pizza and not any pizza but Artichoke Pizza. I bought 3 slices and paid the employee by credit card and I also left a $2 cash tip. The pizza was amazing as always. Fast forward a few days later when I received the credit card charge for $2 more than the bill. You might be thinking it's only $2 but that's $2 that was stolen after I already left a $2 cash tip that I didn't have to leave as it's an order and go establishment not a sit down dining experience. On Monday, March 27 I spoke with the manager who at first wanted me to believe it was an honest mistake but realized that I wasn't letting this go and so he agreed to refund me the $2. I went after work from Midtown to the 14th street location only to find that the manager had already left for the day. I spoke with another employee who was aware of the situation and explained that the manager had credited me the full amount. I called my credit card company before leaving Artichoke and was told it could take a week or more before I would receive the credit. It is now April 6 and no credit has been received. So not only was I the victim of being stolen from but also lied to and inconvenienced by the store manager. Again, the pizza is amazing but do you want to support an establishment that steals from and lies to its customers? I for one do not. Attached is a photo of the receipt and my credit card charge.

- Category: Value for Money
    - Description: Overcharged by $2, leading to dissatisfaction regarding the value for money received.

Name: Simon M.
Comment:
Came at 5pm on Thursday and it was empty so ideal timing, since this place is normally packed. Servers were friendly and I had a lovely chat while business was a bit slow. Interior was cool - the kitchen is open so you can see them make the pizzas. There are no seats, only standing table shelves along the wall.
I ordered a slice of Artichoke pizza since this is what they are known for. I was not expecting something so huge for $7. It was bigger than my head and at least 1.5lbs. The crust was crust, thin and delightfully chewy. The topping was completely unique though. It was like putting an artichoke bechamel on a pizza. It wasn't bland like I get with so many creamy pizza toppings. Great artichoke flavor. I wish there were more pieces of artichoke to add texture. It was also a bit sloppy and fell off the pizza. However, overall it was an amazing and unique slice to try. Just don't expect to be able to eat anything after having one slice lol.

Name: Lea B.
Comment:
I was on a business trip and hurting for some lobster.  After asking around, several locals recommended this place.  Now, I have to tell you, as my co-worker and I drove there, we began to get worried....  we were expecting this place to be a fancy restaurant.  Honestly, it is neither.  But, please note the 4 stars above.
Parking is terrible as you have to park on the street.  When you enter through the door you are greater by a counter and 2 large circular lobster tanks.  You can literally pick out your lobster from a vat of sorts, which we did!  We also ordered the lobster mac and cheese.  Oh, I forgot to mention the "artwork" on the walls... lots of painted images/murals.
Then we were told to go next door and wait.  So, feeling weirdly uncomfortable about it, we left to wait.  They DO NOT sell alcohol, so we headed to a corner store/bodega and bought some deuce deuces!!!!  Headed back and took a seat at the pick-nick tables on an indoor concrete floor.  Low and behold our food arrived on plastic trays several minutes later.  It was hot and plentiful and all the usual lobster tools were provided.  Bibs, Crackers, and Picks!
I did not care for the pick-nick tables you sit at or the fact that this places has 2 separate entrances making it confusing.
Lastly, bathrooms we clean and looked recently renovated. 
Overall.... yummy! But not a sit down restaurant as you might expect.

Comment:
Red Hook Lobster Pound's signature item is its lobster roll. They have a few different variations priced at $24 each. We opted for the Connecticut style lobster roll. It was delicious but a little too buttery. The amount of bread was perfect which allowed the lobster meat to stand out. We chose to have sweet potato fries with our lobster roll. I'd definitely recommend them as a side.
We also had some clam chowder with our lobster roll. The soup had a balanced consistency and was well seasoned. There was plenty of clams!
The lobster pound has a great selection of craft and local beers including offerings from Sixpoint, Rockaway, and Allagash. They offer Allagash White which is one of the best beer pairings for seafood.
The service was quick and friendly. The restroom is wildly spacious; there is room for three beach chairs plus plenty of extra space.

Comment:
When you first step into this small cozy restaurant, you feel as if you've stepped out of Brooklyn and into a small shack in cape cod. 
The prices are not cheap, but no surprise here since 1. This is nyc and 2. You're at a lobster joint. 
We got the $75 meal for two- which includes appetizer, two cups of soup and two lobster rolls.
The New England clam chowder was thin and slightly watery. Normally I like a thick and heavier chowder. Despite this, we mustered the courage to continue in our culinary journey here.
The lobster rolls were out of this world good. They went light on the mayo and heavy on the lobster. Roll was not too buttery or greasy, and romaine at the bottom of the roll was a nice addition. French fries were also done surprisingly well. 
While the prices aren't cheap, if you come for the lobster rolls, this place is a slam dunk and you won't be disappointed, just wouldn't recommend the New England chowder.

Thank you for coming. I am sorry that you did not like the chowder, it usually is thicker. I will look into it! Glas you enjoyed the lobster.

Name: Kenny C.
Comment:
Red Hook Lobster Pound is just swell. They were badly affected by Hurricane Sandy, but the owners used it as an opportunity to renovate their space and offer a more full dining experience. To that end, it looks like they've definitely succeeded.
We were able to just walk in on a Sunday night and grab a table. Our waitress apologized for the wait, even though it was literally 20 seconds.
Started out with some beers and a cup of the New England chowder, which was possibly the best I've had since I was in Boston years ago. Their beer selection is also pretty good. Lots of crafts.
We wanted to do the oysters Rockafella, but sadly, they were sold out for the day. Also sold out of the peel and eat shrimp. Sad face.
Oh well, that just meant we had to get more lobster! We split the lobster mac and cheese. The lobster chunks were oh so plentiful. With some fresh pepper from the grinder, it was perfect.
For my lobster roll, I went with Connecticut style. So buttery, with giant chunks of fresh, meaty lobster. About as good as it gets. The slaw on the side was decent, and I opted for potato salad, which was pretty good.
Of course, no review of the newly renovated Lobster Pound would be complete without reviewing the bathroom in the back. It's big and round, with a beach umbrella mounted to the ceiling, three beach cots on the ground, and mirrors all around (kind of like the funhouse sort). Definitely the coolest bathroom I've been to in recent memory (and I like bathrooms).
Stop by and show these guys some love!

Name: Raye S.
Comment:
I am very particular about the places that I patronize. A lot of places in new Brooklyn are overpriced, have mediocre food and terrible customer service. Red Hook Lobster Pound surpassed all my expectations and the Down East Chowdah was one of the best soups I've had this year. 
-- On to the specifics
Came here after my fiance and I passed it on the way to the Brooklyn Crab. He really wanted a lobster roll and Brooklyn Crab did not scratch that itch for him so we ended up coming here the next day. I was put off at first at the prices ($25 for a roll) and wasn't actually going to get a lobster roll but once his arrived I had second guessed my decision. 
I ordered the fried lobster roll but the one I received was extremely salty, I was extremely disappointed and hadn't eaten all day so I ended up sending it back to the kitchen. I was apologized to profusely by multiple members of the staff and it was explained someone got a little heavy handed with the salt. I was asked if I wanted something else and ended up ordering the roll my guy had(Connecticut​ Roll). It was delicious, super meaty and tasty. They did not charge us for any lobster rolls. The fact that the staff was super diligent and apologetic made this experience THE BEST experience I've had at similar places in Brooklyn.
OH, THEY ALSO HAVE A LOBSTER SPECIAL ON WEDS!
Reply Content:
Thanks Raye. I am sorry that the fried lobster rol was salty and I do hope that you will come again.

Name: Dave K.
Comment:
I was biking past Madison Square Park today and I noticed the "Eat at Madison Sq. Park" booths, so I stopped by. I decided to make my way to the Red Hook Lobster Pound booth and ordered a Maine style lobster roll. Granted, I'm still new to lobster rolls (only had about 3 before this), but this is the best lobster roll I've ever eaten.
First, the lobster was outstanding - nice big chunks of lobster, cooked perfectly - not chewy or rubbery at all. The lemon mayo it was dressed in was just enough to compliment the lobster without overpowering it. Finally, the bread was great, the outside lightly browned in butter.
It's now four hours later and I'm seriously contemplating going back there to order another. Definitely will stop by their restaurant next time I'm near Red Hook.

Name: Arnold R.
Comment: As a birthday gift, I decided to take my father here because he loves lobster. My dad gave the lobster dinner an 8 out of 10, hence the four stars. My mother is not a fan of seafood, so she got the Hook Burger. She did not like the taste of the patty and the large amount of meat in it. However, she enjoyed the Ice Cold Brew Organic Coffee. I wanted to try out their clam chowder but unfortunately it does not come in a bread bowl. Instead, I ordered Fish and Chips, which was good. The service was great. Our waitress stopped by at our table a few times to ensure everything was okay. I was happy Red Hook Lobster Pound made my dad's birthday even better!

Name: Greg D.
Comment:
Ok, finally hit this lobster joint  which was on my bucket list for some time now last week on a 9/7/22 Thursday around 3:pm or so.
Parking could be a lil tuff at times but we were lucky as we pulled up a car pulled out so that was easy ... 
Loved the inside dining room & bar area very comfortable not fancy nice a simple; the place was still quite only three tables or so ...
The server / bartender was very polite and gave us her recommendations; we opted for 2 - Connecticut Lobsters rolls @ $ 32 bucks each for a total of $ 64 bucks plus we took a Piña colada frozen drink also to go.. total $ 78.50 plus a $ 6 tip ya know lobster ya gotta pay !! 
Our order was ready to go in less then 10 minutes or so; we then ran down to our favorite BBQ Joint Home Town to get our BBQ Meat on, a lil Surf & Turf !! 
The lobster Roll; 1 roll had very nice size chunks & claw meat loaded real nice; Roll 2 was a bit of a disappointment no large chunks of lobster meat all tiny pieces. Also in my opinion not enough butter on both rolls also we would have like our roll / bun toasted a lil more ... 
Very fresh it was good at best; We frequently patronize Luke's lobster roll in Manhattan and in my opinion and my wife's Lukes blows them away......
Would I come back, no I don't think so; their lobsters rolls are supposed to be the star of their show  and they didn't deliver .... 
Do I recommend, only if Luke's goes out of business or if ya live in the area ...
Thanks, 
Greg D & family

When I went I was in a group of 4, so we ordered 4 rolls, 2 Connecticut and 2 Maine style, and shared them. They were just ok, edible, but nothing special. I love seafood, especially lobster and I know that it's pricey but I don't think the 21.00 price tag per lobster roll was justifiable. It didn't taste like very good quality lobster, maybe if there were more seasonings on it to distract from the taste? But then, what's the point if you want to taste the lobster, right? Also, one of the rolls came with a pickle that tasted rotten which was gross and made the whole experience even more disappointing. I wouldn't go back again. There are better places to go to get my seafood fix.

Name: Patrick W.
Comment:
I am going to go with Red Hook Lobster Pound over Luke's. This place is a bit far to get to but there's a wide variety of goodness at this little spot.
For starters, you can go with the Maine or Connecticut style. Cold with mayo or hot with butter. Both choices are great so I usually get one of each. The buns here are toasty and so buttery inside. If you're going with a group, have one person hold the seating down. There's a separate dining room next door you can chow down at.
If it's your first time, try one of each. The lobster mac and cheese here is pretty darn good. Pasta shells with lots of melted cheese (they use 4 types) and tender chunks of robster.
The bisques and chowder are pretty good too. Steve's Key Lime Pie, just the right size to finish the lobster fest.
P.S. - Really random but the Tuna Melt here is off the chain.

Name: Catherine M.
Comment:
I've been here several times and the lobsters are meaty, cooked perfectly, and worth the 30 minute walk from the train station... until today we though. I showed up with a friend today and we were pissed to see the place closed. I called and snapped at them for not updating their Yelp post. A minute later while my friend and I were still standing outside, searching for another place to have lunch, lady walked out and asked if we had just called and that she wanted to give us gift certificates for a lobster roll for the inconvenience. Already, my friend and I felt a bit sheepish but were thankful for the gesture. And then the owners appeared and told us that it was impossible for them to close the hours on their Yelp post without appearing to be permanently closed and that they were closed because of the damages done by hurricane Sandy. 
:X
The gift certificates were not necessary and Michelle and I felt like ignoramus idiots for only remembering then that Red Hook was submerged in water during the devastation. We will definitely continue to recommend Red Hook as having the best lobster plate and rolls we ever had in New York City. They serve their lobsters in other venues throughout NYC as well so check them out. The price is lower than most establishments and fresh and delicious.

Name: Joanne U.
Comment:
We did a special date night this past Saturday and made it a little trip from Uptown Manhattan by taking the ferry from Pier 11, turned out to be fun! Not to mention it's always a nice excuse to enjoy our world famous skyline. 
I've bookmarked this place for awhile now and was excited to finally try it. Couldn't get reservations via Resy anymore so tried the walk-in route and was given a great table by the patio. First sign that things are gonna be good when you see a really diverse staff welcoming you nicely. Covid-19 procedures in place are taking your temperatures before being seated and excellent social distancing among tables.
As for the food, my goodness, we wanted to try it all! We do IF so this was our only meal for the day and we decided to indulge. We got a bottle of Prosecco, which was perfectly chilled and was also put in a wine bottle chiller, really appreciated that. We ordered the Hot Crab Dip with potato chips, this was really good! The Calamari was also good with a mayo dip and another spicy chili dip, just wish it had more crisp in it. The Connecticut Lobster Rolls were amazing and was truly what we were craving for. Delicious, perfectly seasoned, and more importantly, generous with lobster meat, which we felt was a good value at $26 each. The fries that came with it were also perfect! We also ordered a lobster dinner to share which was at $31 each. This was expectedly good for a lobster, but honestly thought we found better value getting the lobster roll.
Overall, we loved almost everything, the staff and service was also great! Will be happy to go back anytime soon. My husband and I were laughing that we actually ended up spending more here than at Peter Luger, but hey, as long as we get incredible value for what we pay for, right? Highly recommend! So glad we have this place for when the New England feel and craving strikes! Thanks, guys!

Name: Antonella B.
Comment:
Look I can only answer this in question format... 
Would I drive to Brooklyn just to come here? No I would not. 
Would I voluntarily walk around this neighborhood to get here? Again, a big fat no. 
Is there a train nearby? Sorta but have a high tolerance of walking through some serious ghetto housing. 
Would I drive here and easily be able to find parking? Absolutely. 
Would I order the Maine Style Lobster Roll or the Connecticut Style Roll (hot and drizzled with butter)? Definitely CT Style, the butter is the very essence of a roll and the smell that draws you into the pound. 
Do I love lobster bisque? An understatement. 
Do I love this lobster bisque? NOO, don't get me wrong they didn't so much as skimp on meat but the bisque part itself was watery and more soupy. (Spoiled by Luke's Lobster's Bisque) 
Who has more lobster in their roll Red Hook's or Luke's? Definitely this place, sorry Luke but at least you still have location. 
Is $15 appropriate pricing for this lobster roll? YESS!! 
Are desserts available? Yes, heavy duty whoppie pies but no I didn't try it.. I was too busy thinking about ordering a second lobster roll. 
Is seating available? Yes, however I prefer to scarf down lobster in my car, the classy way. 
Will I be back? Sure, the staff was friendly and anywhere that smells like butter is okay in my book.

Name: James M.
Comment: This is probably my favorite to go to spot for lobster rolls. I visited this establishment since I was at a nearby IKEA and seen this restaurant had a lot of good reviews. Parking in this area can be a task, however, I was lucky enough to find parking right out front. Upon entering I noticed they have a large number of seatings as well as a few outside if you wanted to get some sun while you grub. They also have specials from 3pm-6pm Monday-Friday $1 oysters as well as $6 draft beers which are quite the bargain. I was seated very quickly and ordered the Connecticut lobster roll since I wanted something warm. I usually would get a main classic lobster roll which is cold when I go to other lobster roll establishments but decided to try something different. It came with French fries and coleslaw. It was a little pricey at $26 however compared to other lobster roll joints they really pack that roll for that price which is a huge thumbs up. The french fries were very salty and I was not a fan of that coleslaw. Otherwise, If you are ever in the Red Hook area of Brooklyn I would definitely advise you to pop in this spot for a quick bite.

Comment: Went there for brunch on Saturday with my husband around 1:30pm. It was a bit crowded but luckily we got a table right away. We ordered an appetizer to share, fried calamari, two classic lobster rolls, a soda and a beer. Our drinks came fast even though we had to ask for some water as well, which I thought they should have brought it to us regardless we ordered soda and beer. Anyway, our food took over 30 mins to arrive. In fact three tables that sat after us got their food first, and we were still waiting. I asked the waitress and she said the kitchen was busy but I pointed out that other people got their food even though they sat and ordered after us. Long story short our food finally came and my lobster roll was cold, while on the menu it said it was supposed to be warm. I asked to have a new one that was warm and she made it happen without waiting too long, plus she offered to give us another beer for free. Because of this and because the food was actually good I decided to give 4 stars instead of 2 but they should work on how they manage their orders.

Comment:
If these people are smart, they'll stop selling lobster rolls at the entrance to Brooklyn Bridge Park. Because:
1) they're not that good (assuming they're better in Red Hook)
2) do you really want a plastic-gloved teenager to reach into his mouth before he assembles your lobster roll by hand?
2a) do you really want a plastic-gloved teenager to reach into his mouth before he assembles your lobster roll by hand while another employee (his mom?) watches him and says nothing?
2b) do you really want to watch a plastic-gloved teenager pull lobster pieces out of his mouth and exclaim, "Yuck! Finny!" before he assembles your lobster roll?
2c) lobsters don't have fins.
3) a commercial hot dog bun is fine but why did you toast it three weeks ago?
A lobster roll should cost a lot because it's made out of lobster. A lobster roll should be really good.
One for two. I think you know which.

Name: Rebecca C.
Comment:
So on my last visit to Brooklyn, my family was discussing lobster rolls, and my dad asked, "what is that like sushi?" My brother nearly spat his water out at my father in shock! Apparently, he either had never had one or had one so lackluster he couldn't remember it. It was then my brother declared we must all visit the Red Hook Lobster Pound for lobster rolls.
I was quickly impressed by the small menu showing they know what they are doing & do it well. They have 3 types and we each tried a different one. They all looked pretty similar (see all the lovely yelp pictures) and they tasted better than imaginable. The prices seemed to go up based on yelp pictures of the old menu and were closer to $20 each, and they are small so I recommend getting a side or appetizer. 
The mussels as an appetizer were awesome, perfectly baked and flavored. The desserts were also phenomenal and we fought over the last shared bits. I can't wait to go back here with friends or the hubby! If you want a lobster roll, this is where to get it!

The portion size is now smaller than it used to be.   On the plus side, the quality remains high.    Staff are still nice.
Reply Content:
Hi Jared,
Thanks so much for your visit and for your feedback. We can assure you that we're still the same mom and pop team that we've always been, but we have to keep up with the changing costs of everything that goes into running a business--including the lobster. We only buy the best, and that often costs more than the rest.
We do hope that you understand and that you pay us another visit in the future--come by on a Wednesday and get a full lobster dinner for only $28!
Thanks again!
Susan

Name: Roy R.
Comment: I've had lobster rolls all over the northeast. If it's on the menu, there's a better chance than not that I'll order it. Basically I'm saying I've had some lobster rolls in my day and Red Hook Lobster Pound is my favorite. It's just so perfect. To be fair I know it's hard to mess up a lobster roll. It's a delicious concoction by its very nature. The traditional Maine Style Lobster Roll, which is the only one I can comment on because it's the only one I keep getting, does the simple things so right. The bun is toasted and buttery. The lobster is lightly dressed so the fresh sweet lobster is without question the lead in the play. Speaking of lobster, I know it's advertised to be a quarter pound per roll, but it feels like way more. The lobster to bun ratio feels like 19 to 1. This is one of the qualities I like most about it, to be quite honest. I've been to Luke's and that famous cliffside lobster joint in Portland, Maine and I still heavily prefer Red Hook. I really can't even resist getting it when I pass by Urbanspace. The lobster roll is the best tasting, best value because it is unapologetically stuffed with lobster, and most memorable. Plus the sides are great too. I always get, and recommend you always get, the sweet potato fries with a side of chipotle mayo. I've recommended Red Hook Lobster Pound on more occasions that I can even count. The best lobster, and best seafood in general, is not at some jacket and tie establishment somewhere in Manhattan. It's at a quirky, unpretentious joint in Brooklyn called Red Hook Lobster Pound.

Comment:
I'm not really a lobster kind of person...I usually go for crab...but, we trekked all the way to brooklyn flea to sample what had been touted as the #1 lobster roll in nyc.
In all honesty?  I was pretty excited.  Luckily we arrived before the lunch rush.  For $15 you get your lobster roll (maine or connecticut style), a bag of chips and a pickle wedge.  Lobster roll is packed full of tender, juicy claw meat with a super light coating of mayo it's barely even there.  All is on a crispy buttery toasted bun (OMG soo yummy) and the chips compliment this roll perfectly.
Once you have your food, head over to a nice shaded spot on the steps and dig in...It's a lot more filling than you'd think, so start off with one and then get another if you really have the room for it.  We tried maine style but maybe we'll go for connecticut style next time.

Name: Mariya G.
Comment:
When we arrived there we discovered that we had to order in one place and then bring the food to the dining room next door. That could be a cool concept if the dining room is nice and has a killer view, like many of the seafood shacks in Cape Cod. Red Hook Lobster Pond has a fairly small cafeteria style dining room with long wooden picnic tables and benches. The only windows are by the entrance leading to the street outside. No veranda, no garden, no backyard, no water view. Just a very loud, cavernous dining room.
The place where orders are taken is also very loud. The cashier kept asking us to repeat ourselves and still left something off the order.
The prices are pretty high for a semi-self-service place. Full service places in Sheepshead Bay have comparable prices. So, I don't get why this place is so popular.
The food is decent, I must say. The lobster is super fresh!
We ordered a lobster bisque, which was tasty but very spicy -- something that the menu does not mention. The lobster rolls were really tiny. The one called Cape Cod didn't really taste like the ones we had in Cape Cod, and we had many. It was almost unseasoned. However, the other rolls were quite delicious.
All in all, the next time I'm craving lobster I will not be heading to Red Hook.

Name: Chelsea C.
Comment:
The food was pretty good but the wait and service was just too slow. We got there at 8pm on a Friday and sat at the bar. We decided to eat at the bar. People around us waited over 20 minutes to get the bartenders attention to get a drink. We got the calm chowder which was warm but not hot. It was very tasty and it was good temp to eat right away but I feel like it may have been sitting in the kitchen a while. 
I ordered fish and chips and my bf got the lobster roll. It was super busy so we figured it would be a wait to get food. We waited an hour and 15 min for the wrong order to come out. Two lobster rolls. They took back all the food and we waited another 30 min for the bartender to tell us that the reason the food was taking so long was because they didn't have any potato salad left.. so after two hours we got the correct order. Again the food was pretty good and the portion was fair but I didn't really want to eat dinner at 10pm... 
Maybe I would come back at a time that wasn't so popular to give it another try.
Reply Content:
Chelsea,
I am truly embarrassed and sorry about your experience. May 4th was a very difficult service for us. The Five Boro Bike race moved their venue to Red Hook at the last minute and we had an extra 30,000 people in the neighborhood. Pioneer Works had an art fair that let our at 8 pm, which brought even more people into our neighborhood.  Red Hook is a small community with a limited number of eating establishments. We tried to bring in more staff but our employees had rightfully made their plans and many were not able to come in. We had 200% more customers than we were expecting and when that happens systems get strained an clearly ours were not working. We care deeply about our customers, our food and our service. Please do come back and give us a try. All I can do is make this public apology which I am going to repeat to all the folks who had this experience. Please ask for me personally if you come in again and I will do my best to make it up to you. 
Susan

Name: Stacey T.
Comment:
We came here earlier in the season because we were around the corner picking up our 5 boro bike tour registration packs. 
I'm updating my review because although we had an enjoyable meal, the size and quality of the roll was far superior when I last visited over the summer of 2017 (my pictures clearly show the differences between the 2 rolls).  The lump crab dip was also lacking "lump crab," I wouldn't order it again. The New England clam chowder was VERY good and I'd definitely order it again. If you're going with a friend the "Maine Event" for $75 is a great deal!
Reply Content:
Stacey,
I am sorry that you did not have an exceptional experience. At this time of year we are dealing with hard shell lobster and it is simply not as sweet as the summer lobster. Also if you came May 4, it was an insane night as 
the Five Boro Bike race moved their venue to Red Hook at the last minute and we had an extra 30,000 people in the neighborhood. Pioneer Works had an art fair that let our at 8 pm, which brought even more people into our neighborhood.  Red Hook is a small community with a limited number of eating establishments.  We care deeply about our customers, our food and our service. Please do come back and give us a try. Our food is ordinarily exceptional at the restaurant. All I can do is make this public apology which I am going to repeat to all the folks who had this experience. Please ask for me personally if you come in again and I will do my best to make it up to you. 
Susan

Name: Matthew S.
Comment:
Uh oh, yes. This is going to be one of those breakdown reviews, also known as my longer reviews, where I break down everything I ordered and give it a score.
Bear in mind I have been to Maine, if you have followed me long enough you know that. While there I ate many lobster rolls, tried the soups, and know the seafood. Check those out if you have time. 
Lobster Bisque: It lacks here. No chunks of lobster, just creamy in texture with fine lobster bits. It is pretty small for the price, and was not sad when I finished it. The fact it came with oyster crackers helped. Score: 3/5
Lobster Mac n Cheese: I got the small and it was more than enough. The sauce is creamy, the noodles are perfectly cooked, and it is plenty rich. It could use more lobster meat and some breadcrumbs to add some crunch to the dish. However, once the lobster was eaten, it was too much noodle, and I had to discard it. Maybe I could have kept the leftovers in post, but then it is just a Mac n cheese, which I could have gotten anywhere else. Score 3.5/5
Classic Roll: This is the knuckles and claws of this review, and yes that is deliberate. There is a lot of lobster meat on the roll. It is succulent and fresh, and the roll is authentic. Two problems, the lemon flavor is overwhelming on the roll, and the roll has lettuce in it, and scallions. WHY? Score: 3.5/5
Coleslaw: This comes with the roll, but I did not like it one bit. It is dry and bland and brings zero value. I was better off ordering chips instead. Score: 1/5
Connecticut Roll: Funny enough, this was the roll I really enjoyed. Drawn butter, succulent meat, no lettuce (atta boy!), fresh flavors. The joke is that because we are closer to Connecticut the roll would taste better, but in all seriousness, it is the more authentic roll. Simple does it, and that is what happens here. Score: 4/5
Lemonade: A little tart for my liking, but not too bad. Could have been worse, but also better. I should have gotten one of those Maine sodas as I know they have a bit of bite to them. This was pleasant and mellow. Score: 3/5
Before I left I spoke with one of the managers and he was a chill dude. He took all my suggestions seriously and was impressed by my knowledge of Maine desserts (Blueberry pies and Whoopie pies please!), and that if they were on the dessert menu, I would have certainly ordered them.
I guess I will have to come back in the summer to see if it really feels like a taste of Maine.
Reply Content:
Hey Matthew, its Kerry we spoke at the restaurant. i Would like to once again thank you for all of your feedback. We appreciate the constructive feedback as much if not more than being praised. 
We are already in talks to make changes to a few of the things you listed so hopefully next time you come by we can knock it out of the park. 
Whoopie pies are now in house and being sold.
Thanks once again
-Kerry

Name: Lisa C.
Comment: My husband and I came here for his early birthday celebration since it has good reviews. We ordered the Maine event, which might cost more than ordering individually or saves you a few dollars depending on what you choose. We did the Maine event which comes with one appetizer, 2 cups of soup or salad and 2 entree. We did two different soup, the new England chowder wasn't anything special but the down east chowder was different. We got the fried oysters which wasn't bad either. The disappointing part was the lobster rolls. I got the Connecticut and my husband got the classic. The Connecticut is suppose to be with poached butter but I did not taste anything. There was no butter taste or paprika, definitely lacking spice and flavor. My husband got the classic and he said it was okay as well, also not much flavor. He said the best thing was the pickles and fries. Overall, for a $100 dinner, it was just mediocre and lacking flavor. I prefer burger & lobster or Luke's lobsters. I am not sure if we went on a off day but probably wouldn't give it another try.

Name: Harsh S.
Comment:
I finally tried the famous Di Fara Pizza and now understand why it is ranked among the best. They serve the most thin and crispy slice with almost no flop. They top it with a sauce that perfectly balances salty and savory, pleasing your taste buds with every bite. I am not sure how they get the perfect amount of cheese on there, but the cheese did not dominate the slice  yet it complimented the sauce and crust so well. I like the fact that everything they serve comes out of the oven fresh right in front of you. Also, there are not that many toppings or other types of slices to choose from, so it eliminates a lot of decision making.
Though the pizza is amazing, the restaurant is a hole in the wall on a busy Brooklyn street. They have minimal seating on small folding chairs and tables; something you'd expect to see in one of those dollar slice places. It is also difficult to get to the restaurant, especially if you do not live nearby. You can either take the Q train or drive in some of the most narrow and busy streets to get here.
The staff is very friendly and do their best to get your pizza out quick but there can be days where you have to wait in line or wait over an hour to get your pizza, but hey it's really some of the best in the world.
5/5 stars!

Name: Ken S.
Comment:
Yes, I'll admit it. I trust Dave Portnoy's pizza reviews - it's to the point where when I conduct business travel, I'll only stop at places he rates at an 8 or higher. Am I becoming a pizza snob in my old age? Probably. I don't care. I told someone that I've lived in various cities in CA most of my life, and tried plenty of pies there. Taking chains out of the equation (which are mostly inedible), I believe some of the worst NY pizza (which includes New Haven, CT) would rank amongst the best in California. 
My continued pilgrimage to experience pizza excellence this particular trip brought me to Johnny's in Mt. Vernon and Di Fara deep in Brooklyn (Midwood neighborhood).
I tried two slices - one regular and one square. The square one (maybe a grandma slice?) featured a thicker crust with more "heft", while the thinner crust had an extra crunchy crust. Zero flop on both. Pristine undercarriage. 
This place is a bit unique in that it features pieces of ripped basil, which adds a certain subtle boost of flavor. 
I'm normally a pepperoni or sausage guy. However, I wanted to have this one naked - no toppings, just cheese. This is the only true test of a pizza's base level of quality. 
Let me state unequivocally. Johnny's was extremely tasty, but Di Fara was on another level of unf---king-believableness. 
Nirvana. Manna from heaven. Pure bliss. All of it. You need to come here. You should be ashamed of yourself, because you SHOULD'VE been here already.
Dave gave this a 9.4 - I'm throwing down a 9.8. Enjoy.

Name: Jake L.
Comment:
The worst pizza I have had the honor of eating and I'm still fuming about lack of customer service and all the runarounds I'm getting from this estab lishment. They may have been stellar when old timer was still around but now whoever is the pizza maker at the Frontline should shoot themselves in the feet and hands for making possibly the worst pizza I have had not just in Brooklyn but anywhere in NYC. When it arrived at my doorstep I knew something was off when I grabbed the bottom of the box and it wasn't warm but I gave them the benefit of the doubt. Lo and behold, after opening I knew it was a cold ass pizza that's been sitting out for quite some time by the dull color of the toppings and dry and cold temperatures of the ingredients. I attributed it to the deliveryman who took about an hour and change from the order placement to the arrival but of course I knew better that that wasn't the case. Sure enough after I turned them in the air fryer to salvage them my first few bites told the whole story and confirmed my suspicion that this pie has been out in the cold for at least a couple hours with neglect. What was more telling of this neglect was that each slice was mismatched cut of slices from different pies and they put them together to make a whole pie. My family who have no problem knocking out 2 large pies in one sitting ended up eating only half of a pie and threw the rest in the trash. To add to even more frustration, they use a variety of different delivery platforms and we got the runaround and still haven't figured out how to contact them. At first it was the app Sauce then doordash
then gold belly then the restaurant who told us they cannot refund but should have to contact delivery app to request and back and forth, each of them telling us the other party has to sign off on the refund request. Ridiculous and poor customer service and pizza craftsmanship. They should be ashamed of themselves!! I'm telling half my friends in Bensonhurst not to visit this joke of a business.

Name: Matthew L.
Comment:
Di Fara Pizza is a historic pizzeria that opened in Brooklyn, New York in 1965. It was founded by Italian immigrant business partners Domenico "Dominic" De Marco and Franco Farina. De Marco immigrated to New York City from Caserta in 1959. After briefly working on a farm, he began making pizzas with his brother at a spot in Brooklyn's Sunset Park neighborhood. De Marco grew tired of cheap, complaining customers and the neighborhood's crime rate. Someone told him about a location in Brooklyn's Midwood neighborhood and he decided to open a new pizzeria there with Farina. The partners' attorneys wanted a fusion of their names, so the name 'Di Fara' was born. In 1977, Farina was interested in going back to Italy with his family and De Marco bought him out. De Marco worked tirelessly at the pizzeria for several decades alongside his family, with many calling his pizzeria the 'best in Brooklyn'. He passed away in 2022 at the age of 85. Other members of the De Marco family (including daughter Maggie and son Alex) are continuing on his pizza legacy. 
I visited Di Fara this past summer while I was cycling all around New York City. I was checking out some Brooklyn pizzerias and breweries for the first time, and Di Fara was my first stop of the day. I arrived just before they opened and was first in line. When Di Fara opened in 1965, Midwood was a mix of Italian, Greek and Jewish immigrants. Today it is overwhelmingly Orthodox Jewish. I ordered a "Regular Slice" and a "Square Slice". I grabbed a seat by the counter and enjoyed my two slices fresh out of the oven. I started with the regular first. It had delicious sauce, good chew to the crust, and very fresh-tasting basil. This slice set the bar high. Then I tried the square. I don't normally go for square slices (or 'Sicilian-style'), but this one was excellent! The crust had the perfect balance of crunch and chew with the same great sauce, cheese and basil from the regular slice. I think that I may have even enjoyed the square better. This legendary pizzeria totally lives up to the hype! I look forward to returning!

Name: Daniel B.
Comment: Portnoy said this place was a 9.4, making it his best real score, so I knew I had to try it. And despite it being hyped up to no end, it lived up to it. Excellent, excellent pizza. As soon as you see the "menu" scribbled on a paper plate you know you're at a real deal place - and the guy cutting basil off the stem only solidifies that. It's all made to order so you'll wait - we got there at opening and were the second to place our order and it still took 50 minutes to eat, but man was it worth the wait. I've never heard pizza make noise before - until the square pie came out and the bubbling was audible across the pizzeria - but now it's the most beautiful sound I've ever heard. The square slice had such weight - a chewy yet crisp crust, and a great flavor blend between the cheese, sauce, and oil drizzled on top to finish it off.  The crust was just a smidge too burnt on top but otherwise the slice was phenomenal. And the regular slice was equally as great. No flop, crisp undercarriage, and a thin light delicious pizza. I don't care where you live, this place is worth the trek!
